title: Reappraise Anxiety as Excitement
technique: Say out loud 'I am excited' right before you speak instead of trying to calm down. Anxiety and excitement are both high-arousal states, so relabeling is easier than de-escalating, and it shifts you into an opportunity mindset.
source: Alison Wood Brooks, 'Get Excited,' Journal of Experimental Psychology: General 143(3), 2014

title: Long Exhalations to Calm Arousal
technique: Breathe in through the nose, then out longer through the mouth. This lowers physiological arousal and steadies your voice.
source: Julian Treasure, TED; Matt Abrahams, Stanford GSB

title: Don't Memorize — Internalize
technique: Never memorize word-for-word. It raises cognitive load because you're comparing what you meant to say against what you're saying. Instead, learn a roadmap of key ideas and keep exact figures on a note card.
source: Matt Abrahams, Stanford GSB

title: Build Around One Throughline
technique: Anchor the whole talk to a single connecting idea that every point hangs on. Try to state it in 15 words or less. A throughline is not the same as a topic.
source: Chris Anderson, TED Talks: The Official TED Guide to Public Speaking, 2016

title: What? So What? Now What?
technique: Structure any message in three parts: state your point (What?), explain why it matters (So What?), give the call to action (Now What?).
source: Matt Abrahams, Stanford GSB Insights

title: Start Strong (Hook)
technique: Open with a startling fact, a provocative question, or a short story instead of throat-clearing. Attention is highest in the first 10-20 seconds.
source: Toastmasters International; Vanessa Van Edwards, Cues

title: The Power of the Pause
technique: Pause for a few seconds before starting and around key points. Silence gives the audience time to process and makes you look thoughtful and in control.
source: Toastmasters International; Matt Abrahams, Stanford GSB

title: Land the Phrase to Cut Fillers
technique: Finish each thought on a clear out-breath instead of bridging into the next one with 'um' or 'uh.' Filler words spike under cognitive overload, so slowing down here helps.
source: Matt Abrahams, Stanford GSB (Huberman Lab), citing Jerry Weissman

title: Drop Hedges and Up-Talk
technique: Cut phrases like 'I think,' 'sort of,' and rising question-intonation on statements that aren't questions.
source: Matt Abrahams, Stanford GSB

title: Make Sustained Eye Contact
technique: Hold soft eye contact with individuals for about 3-4 seconds at a time, spending the majority of your speaking time in eye contact. Research links this to higher perceived credibility.
source: 'Eye contact: A nonverbal determinant of speaker credibility,' The Speech Teacher 23(1), 1974

title: Use Purposeful Explanatory Gestures
technique: Talk with your hands using gestures that actually illustrate your meaning. The brain gives extra attentional weight to hand gestures.
source: Vanessa Van Edwards, Cues

title: Ethos, Pathos, Logos
technique: Combine credibility (ethos), emotional appeal (pathos), and logic (logos). Keep ethos always high and adjust the pathos/logos ratio to your audience and topic.
source: Aristotle, Rhetoric (foundational rhetoric framework)

title: Reciprocity
technique: Give something of value first, ideally personalized and a little unexpected. People feel an urge to return the favor.
source: Robert Cialdini, Influence

title: The Rule of Three
technique: Group your key points in threes. Triads are rhythmic and easier to remember than lists of two or four.
source: Rhetorical tradition (Aristotle), standard in communication coursework

title: Character + Tension Keeps Attention
technique: Tell character-driven stories with rising tension. Sustained tension holds attention and has been linked to oxytocin release, which builds empathy and trust in listeners.
source: Paul Zak, 'Why Inspiring Stories Make Us React,' PMC4445577

title: Three-Act Transformation
technique: Structure a personal story as setup, then confrontation or struggle, then resolution. The most transformative stories reveal a real flaw or hardship and require genuine candor.
source: Nancy Duarte, Duarte Inc.

title: Ask Rhetorical & Poll Questions
technique: Use questions for effect, quick polls (hand-raise or live poll), or brief thought-experiments to make listening feel two-way. Tell the audience how you want them to respond.
source: Matt Abrahams, Stanford GSB

title: Use 'You' and 'We'
technique: Connect your topic to the audience using inclusive pronouns, even when telling a personal story.
source: Toastmasters International

title: Balance Warmth and Competence
technique: Charisma is a learnable blend of warmth (trust) and competence (capability). The most charismatic people hit the sweet spot of both rather than leaning fully into either.
source: Vanessa Van Edwards, Cues, citing Fiske, Cuddy & Glick, Trends in Cognitive Sciences, 2007

title: Send Deliberate Warmth Cues
technique: Use personal stories, a genuine (slightly slow) smile, listening sounds, leaning in, and open gestures when you're being perceived as cold or intimidating.
source: Vanessa Van Edwards, Cues

title: RASA: Receive, Appreciate, Summarize, Ask
technique: Pay full attention (Receive), make small acknowledging sounds like 'hmm' or 'okay' (Appreciate), summarize what you heard with 'so...' (Summarize), then ask a follow-up (Ask).
source: Julian Treasure, TED; Stanford GSB 'Four Pillars of Active Listening'

title: Be Interested, Not Interesting
technique: Lead with genuine curiosity about the other person instead of trying to perform for them. The best small-talkers ask more than they tell.
source: Rachel Greenwald, via Matt Abrahams, Stanford GSB

title: Escalating Self-Disclosure
technique: Exchange reciprocal, gradually deepening personal questions rather than staying on small talk. Pairs who did this reported far greater closeness than pairs who made small talk for the same amount of time.
source: Aron et al., 'The Experimental Generation of Interpersonal Closeness,' Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin 23(4), 1997

title: Tactical Empathy
technique: Understand and name the other person's feelings and perspective to lower their defensiveness. This is empathy used strategically, not agreement.
source: Chris Voss, Never Split the Difference

title: Labeling
technique: Name the emotion you observe: 'It sounds like...' or 'It seems like...' Labeling a negative emotion tends to defuse it; labeling a positive one reinforces it.
source: Chris Voss, Never Split the Difference

title: Paraphrase Every Question First
technique: Restate each question in your own words before answering. It buys you thinking time, confirms you understood correctly, and lets you reframe if needed.
source: Matt Abrahams, Stanford GSB

title: Look Into the Camera, Not the Screen
technique: Direct your gaze at the camera lens rather than the screen to create the illusion of eye contact. Position notes or slides close to the camera.
source: Matt Abrahams, Stanford GSB, '10 Tips for Giving Effective Virtual Presentations'

title: Trust Before Competence
technique: Lead with warmth and trustworthiness before showcasing competence. People subconsciously ask 'Can I trust this person?' before 'Can I respect this person?' — and trust comes first.
source: Amy Cuddy, Presence, 2016; 'Connect, Then Lead,' Harvard Business Review, 2013

title: Brevity Conveys Conviction
technique: Use fewer words. Over-explaining tends to signal insecurity, while concise answers project confidence and competence.
source: Matt Abrahams, Stanford GSB (Think Fast, Talk Smart, with Irv Grousbeck)
